items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The AI Monoculture: Market Has Only One Lens Left
items.idea: The equity market has developed what The Dark Side of the Boom calls 'one look syndrome' — AI is the answer to every question regardless of macro regime. Hawkish Fed? Buy AI. Peace dividend? Buy AI. Geopolitical risk? Buy AI. Rates stabilizing? Buy AI. This creates a dangerous concentration where the cost of the first earnings miss rises exponentially. The rally does not need a recession to wobble — it only needs the biggest source of incremental confidence to stop accelerating.
items.angle: The specific risk is not a crash — it is deceleration. When every marginal dollar of market confidence depends on the same thesis accelerating, even a stable (not negative) quarter from AI capex leaders can trigger multiple compression.
items.tweetA: The market has one answer to every question: AI. Hawkish Fed? Buy AI. Peace deal? Buy AI. Geopolitical risk? Buy AI. It's not a thesis anymore — it's a religion. And religions are only as strong as their last miracle.
items.tweetB: A one-lens market is not the same as a wrong market. The railway boom was real. The infrastructure was built. The problem: most railway stocks still went to zero. Are we pricing the infrastructure or the returns on it?

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Warsh Fed: Every Data Release Is Now an Open Auction
items.idea: The new Fed under Kevin Warsh has removed the hand-holding. Without the central bank cushioning through communication, economic releases become 'open auctions' rather than routine checkpoints. Liquidity will be thinner around data, price discovery less orderly, and the first move after a number 'more violent than the final move.' Markets are trying to 'find their footing while everyone is running at once.'
items.angle: This week's PCE is not just about whether inflation is falling — it is whether it is falling fast enough for a Fed that has 'rediscovered its inflation-fighting instincts.' In that uncertainty, the reaction to the number matters more than the number itself.
items.tweetA: The old Fed warned you before every move. The Warsh Fed doesn't. PCE on Friday is no longer a checkpoint — it's an open auction. Liquidity will be thin. First moves will be violent. The edge is in the second move, not the first spike.
items.tweetB: Kevin Warsh changed one thing: the Fed no longer telegraphs. That change means wider spreads on macro data, less orderly price discovery, more violent first moves, and a reaction function markets are still reverse-engineering.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Micron Earnings Are This Week's AI Referendum
items.idea: Memory has become the cleanest test of whether the datacenter spending boom is commercial reality or market mythology. Micron's commentary on high-bandwidth memory pricing, margins, and order visibility will be read as a verdict on the entire AI capex supercycle. Strong HBM data reinforces the buildout narrative; a cautious outlook would 'matter far beyond one semiconductor company' — it would shake the confidence underlying the entire AI equity multiple.
items.angle: Micron is where datacenter clients commit real dollars 12-18 months forward. If they are pulling back, you see it in memory before you see it in hyperscaler capex guidance. This is not a $MU event. It is a $NVDA $MSFT $META event disguised as one.
items.tweetA: Micron this week is not just an earnings call. It's a referendum on whether the AI capex supercycle is real. HBM pricing, margins, order visibility — if these wobble, this isn't a $MU problem. It's a $NVDA $MSFT $META problem.
items.tweetB: Why Micron matters more than it should: memory is where datacenter clients commit real dollars 12-18 months forward. If they're pulling back you see it here before hyperscaler capex guidance. Watch the HBM commentary specifically.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Treasury 2s/5s/7s Auctions: Weak Bid Instantly Becomes a Tech Valuation Event
items.idea: 2-, 5-, and 7-year Treasury supply lands this week in a rates market already trying to work out how much term premium, inflation risk, and policy uncertainty to carry. A weak auction is not just a bond market story — it can 'quickly become a valuation story for technology, a dollar story for FX, and a headwind for the entire long-duration equity complex.' Critically: when valuations are stretched, yields do not need to explode — they only need to stop falling.
items.angle: AI equities are priced for a structural discount rate that is no longer obviously available. Every basis point of term premium repricing is a direct multiple compression event for $QQQ. The mechanism is more direct than most appreciate.
items.tweetA: This week: 2s, 5s, 7s all hitting the market. In a normal rates environment — who cares? But when tech multiples assume low rates forever, a soft auction bid isn't a bond problem. It's a $QQQ problem. Watch the tail.
items.tweetB: Treasury auction supply landing the same week as PCE with a Fed that has no guidance playbook. When valuations are stretched, yields don't need to explode to matter — they only need to stop falling. That's the week ahead in one sentence.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Oil: War Premium Eroding But Hormuz Is Still a Live Left Tail
items.idea: The peace premium is being priced out of crude as markets see a path to more normal physical flows. But this is 'not a clean peace dividend' — it is a fragile bridge. Oil will trade like 'a door on a loose hinge: broadly opening toward lower prices, yet still capable of slamming shut whenever the geopolitical wind changes.' JD Vance heads to Switzerland for the Iran peace process; Steve Witkoff is the 'velvet glove,' Vance the 'harder-edged counterpart.'
items.angle: The key distinction is between a renewed headline spike and genuine return of structural scarcity. A Hormuz closure is not a $10 move — it is a global inflation repricing event. The option premium on that tail is not being priced.
items.tweetA: Oil pricing Hormuz risk almost out. JD Vance flying to Switzerland to close the Iran deal. But ask: what's the option value on being wrong? Hormuz closure isn't a $10 move. It's a global inflation repricing. That tail is not priced.
items.tweetB: Oil 'door on a loose hinge' — broadly opening lower, capable of slamming shut on a wind change. The war premium is eroding, not gone. Separate a headline spike from structural scarcity return. Two completely different trades for $VLO $MPC vs $XOM.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Core PCE Is the Biggest Event for Gold Markets This Week
items.idea: Stephen Innes, Managing Partner at SPI Asset Management: 'With the Fed now appearing more adaptable to changing circumstances and increasingly sensitive to upcoming inflation data, every important economic data release will have an impact, but the core PCE will be a key event for both the gold and interest rate markets, and next week will be highly data-dependent.' Under Warsh, the real-rate path is being repriced. Hot PCE confirms hawkish hold. Cold PCE delivers relief rally.
items.angle: Gold is the cleanest reaction-function trade this week. Unlike equities (which have the AI narrative to hide behind), gold has no cushion. Every basis point of real rate movement is a direct mark-to-market event.
items.tweetA: Everyone is watching PCE for equities. Gold traders know the real bet: Warsh's Fed is newly hawkish AND newly unpredictable. Hot PCE = hawkish hold confirmed = real rate competition for gold. Cold PCE = relief rally. Gold is the cleanest reaction-function trade this week.
items.tweetB: 'Core PCE will be key event for both the gold and interest rate markets' — Stephen Innes, SPI Asset Management. Not stocks. Not bonds. Gold first. Under Warsh, the real-rate path is being repriced from scratch. Nobody has a good model for it yet.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: GS Top Trader: Iran Peace Was Not the Pfizer Moment for Market Broadening
items.idea: Goldman Sachs top trader Louis Miller confirms the rotation out of AI has not occurred. The Iran MOU was expected to be a 'Pfizer vaccine moment' — one geopolitical cloud lifts, rotation into laggards/cyclicals/value follows. Instead, the market 'looked at the peace dividend, nodded politely, and went straight back to the same table where it has been eating all year: AI.' Broadening is happening WITHIN the AI complex, not away from it. AI infrastructure, memory, and inference remain where earnings momentum is strongest.
items.angle: Every rotation catalyst since early 2026 has failed: rates, tariffs, geopolitics, peace deals. At some point the lesson is that the AI monoculture is more entrenched than any traditional macro catalyst can disrupt. GS is now on the record with this call.
items.tweetA: Goldman's top trader says Iran peace was supposed to be the broadening catalyst. Every rotation call this year has failed: tariff relief, rate fear, peace deal. At some point you stop trying to call the rotation and just map the AI complex.
items.tweetB: Iran MOU = expected Pfizer vaccine moment for laggards. Market 'nodded politely and went back to AI.' Louis Miller (GS) on record: rotation out of AI has not occurred. Broadening is happening inside AI, not out of it. This is the positioning read of 2026.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Europe: Long Aerospace/New Defense, Watch Legacy Industrial Get Hollowed Out by China
items.idea: Goldman Sachs (via Dark Side of the Boom) frames the European trade as highly selective: long aerospace and 'new defense,' but the legacy industrial base 'remains exposed to China's export machine.' European defense spending is real and accelerating. But German/French legacy manufacturing — automotive, steel, machinery — faces a Chinese export tsunami now that US tariffs have partially redirected China's surplus toward European markets.
items.angle: This is not a 'buy Europe' trade. It is a barbell: long the new (defense, aerospace, energy transition hardware) and effectively short the old (autos, machinery, steel). The DAX bundles both. Passive index exposure gets you the wrong side.
items.tweetA: The European trade is more specific than 'rearmament = buy DAX.' Aerospace and new defense are real. But German legacy industrial is walking into a Chinese export tsunami. Two completely different stories in the same index. Don't buy the index.
items.tweetB: Europe's legacy industrial base is exposed to China's export machine. Not a tariff story — a structural competitiveness gap. While everyone buys $AIRBUS and defense names, steel, automotive, and machinery are being hollowed out.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Japanese Banks and Asia AI: Cleaner Regional Expressions If Dollar Stays Elevated
items.idea: A hawkish Warsh Fed may strengthen the dollar before it breaks the AI trade, leaving Japanese banks and Asia AI as 'cleaner regional expressions.' Japanese banks benefit from BOJ rate normalization. Asia AI plays (TSMC, SK Hynix, Samsung) offer the same capex cycle without paying US equity valuation multiples. USD/JPY 'can stay elevated for longer than logic suggests, until it suddenly cannot' — and when it moves, Japanese banks re-rate in both directions simultaneously.
items.angle: If Fed-driven dollar strength persists, you want AI exposure without US multiple risk. Asian AI infrastructure and Japanese bank carry are the hedge: same secular tailwind, different entry point, with BOJ normalization as a tailwind rather than headwind.
items.tweetA: If Warsh keeps the dollar elevated, the AI trade gets expensive on a USD-adjusted basis. Japanese banks and Asia AI become cleaner expressions — same secular theme, no US premium to pay. This is the FX-hedged version of the AI trade.
items.tweetB: USD/JPY 'can stay elevated for longer than logic suggests, until it suddenly cannot.' That asymmetry is why Japanese banks are interesting: you collect BOJ normalization carry until the yen moves — at which point the banks re-rate further anyway.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The NewsPlank: Mark the Pre-Release Level, Let the Market Reveal the Real Trade
items.idea: Before any major release, mark the level where the market was trading immediately beforehand — the NewsPlank. Good news that cannot hold above it is bearish. Bad news that rallies back through it is bullish. 'The first move tells you what the models calculated. The movement around the NewsPlank indicates whether actual buyers and sellers agree.' Do not outshoot the algorithms in the first second — let them fire first, then trade the gap between the headline, the details, and market belief.
items.angle: This framework is especially powerful this week because the Warsh Fed's reaction function is unknown. The market does not know how Warsh responds to hot vs cold PCE. The NewsPlank reveals the answer faster than any macro model can.
items.tweetA: Before PCE Friday: mark the pre-release level. That's your NewsPlank. Good number that can't hold above it = bearish. Bad number that rallies back through = bullish. The machines trade the headline. The NewsPlank trades the truth.
items.tweetB: 'The first move tells you what the models calculated. The movement around the NewsPlank indicates whether actual buyers and sellers agree.' Simple. Systematic. Works especially well when the reaction function is unknown — like this week under Warsh.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Gold Could Trade Like a $3 Market on PCE Release — Normal Is $0.05
items.idea: Bank quant contacts are recalibrating pricing engines with wider risk bands, more skew, and sharper news-trading settings ahead of the first real data stress test of the Warsh regime. FX could become 'very thin on majors inside of 20 pips around high-risk releases, while gold could trade more like a $3 market on the releases than a $0.05-10 market as liquidity providers step back and news algos hit the same small pool of available price levels.'
items.angle: This is a risk management alert as much as a trade idea. A 30-60x spread blowout on gold around PCE means execution cost alone can eat your alpha. Size down around the release, or wait for the second move after algorithmic frenzy clears.
items.tweetA: Bank quant contacts recalibrating ahead of PCE. Gold spreads could go from $0.05-10 to $3 on the release. FX thin inside 20 pips on majors. Not a trade alert — a risk management alert. Size down, or don't trade the initial release at all.
items.tweetB: Gold's normal spread: $0.05-0.10. Around a Warsh-era PCE with unknown reaction function: potentially $3. That's 30-60x wider. If you're trading size, the execution cost alone eliminates the alpha. The edge is timing the second move, not the spike.

items.title: Copper: The Next 25 Years May Require As Much As All of Human History Combined
items.idea: Tavi Costa's structural thesis: 'The next 25 years could require as much copper as humanity has produced throughout its entire history.' Every major economic leap (Industrial Revolution, electrical grids, telecom, emerging market urbanization) drove copper demand to previously unimaginable levels. The combination of AI infrastructure (datacenter power + cooling), EVs, grid modernization, and EM electrification now arrives simultaneously — and the mines are not built.
items.angle: New copper mine development takes 10-20 years from discovery to production. The demand curve is inflecting now from multiple sources simultaneously. Supply cannot respond in time. The only variable is when the gap becomes undeniable. $FCX $SCCO $COPX as the leveraged expression.
items.tweetA: 'The next 25 years could require as much copper as humanity has produced throughout its entire history.' — Tavi Costa. If that's even half right, every AI data center, every EV, every grid upgrade is competing for a resource that cannot be printed. $FCX $SCCO $COPX
items.tweetB: Every era of human development had a copper demand surge that seemed impossible before it happened. This time: AI + EVs + grid modernization + EM electrification arriving simultaneously. The mines are not built. The permits are not issued. Supply response is 15 years away.

charts.desc: Tavi Costa copper charts — historical demand vs projected AI/EV/grid demand cycles showing scale of upcoming demand surge vs prior industrial revolutions. CHART WORTH SAVING.

items.title: Hong Kong: The Airport Is Simultaneously the Crime Scene and the Escape Route
items.idea: A cluster of high-value crimes hit HK simultaneously: HK$7M gold bar robbery at the airport (7 arrests, suspects believed across the border); HK$320M illegal online betting syndicate busted (600 officers, 150 arrests, 3 days); HK$27M farm burglary with goods already pawned in Macau before evidence chain could be assembled. Key mechanism: 'disposal speed beats evidence speed.' Gold bars, jewellery, and cash can cross borders faster than police can complete recovery work.
items.angle: The pattern signals that portable stores of value — gold, watches, physical currency — are being weaponized through the HK-Macau-mainland corridor. Prosecution is asymmetrically difficult when the evidence chain requires cross-border cooperation with inherent lag.
items.tweetA: HK airport: simultaneously crime scene and escape route. Gold bars robbed, suspects believed in mainland China within hours. HK$320M betting syndicate busted (600 officers, 150 arrests, 3 days). 'Disposal speed beats evidence speed' is the mechanism. The airport is the choke point.
items.tweetB: Why gold is the perfect crime asset in the HK-Macau-mainland corridor: portable, instantly pawnable, crosses borders as personal property. 6 bars, HK$7M, through an international airport. The enforcement lag is the criminal's structural alpha.

items.title: McKinsey Frames the $2 Trillion Fintech Future
items.idea: McKinsey's Week in Charts featured 'The $2 trillion fintech future' as a key visualization this week. When McKinsey packages a sector with a headline TAM number, it signals the narrative has crossed from venture bet to strategic boardroom priority. The 2022-2023 fintech bloodbath cleared the bad actors. What remains is infrastructure: payments, embedded finance, credit decisioning, and AI-driven underwriting — all being repriced as AI makes the underlying unit economics dramatically better.
items.angle: The fintech repricing from venture darling to strategic infrastructure is a classic second-wave pattern. The boring money (pension funds, strategic acquirers) shows up after the cleanse. The $2T framing from McKinsey — not a VC firm — is the tell that the cleanse is complete.
items.tweetA: McKinsey just put a $2 trillion number on the fintech future. When McKinsey uses that framing, it means the narrative has crossed from venture bet to strategic priority. That's when the boring money shows up — and boring money is patient money.
items.tweetB: The fintech 2022 bloodbath cleared the bad actors. What's left is infrastructure: payments, embedded finance, AI underwriting. McKinsey says $2T TAM. The boring capital has started noticing. Charts inside — worth opening.

charts.desc: Three charts: $2T fintech TAM trajectory, dairy industry priorities, trauma-informed leadership data. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Trauma's Toll on the Workforce: C-Suite Finally Catches Up
items.idea: McKinsey's featured chart this week was 'Trauma's toll on the workforce' with trauma-informed leadership as the management framework. When McKinsey packages something as a chart of the week and a C-suite priority, the institutional conversation has been formalized. The long tail of COVID + political upheaval + AI anxiety + layoff cycles is showing up in workforce productivity data in measurable ways that traditional HR metrics miss.
items.angle: Companies building trauma-informed cultures are solving a retention and productivity problem that pay increases alone cannot fix. The firms that figured this out in 2022-2023 now have a compounding advantage in attrition rates and engagement scores — a competitive moat that shows up in unit economics within 18-24 months.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's chart of the week: 'Trauma's toll on the workforce.' When McKinsey packages psychological safety as a strategic asset, we've crossed a threshold. The companies building trauma-informed cultures aren't being soft — they're solving an attrition problem that pay raises can't fix.
items.tweetB: 5 years of COVID + political volatility + AI anxiety + layoffs = a traumatized workforce. McKinsey is now selling trauma-informed leadership as a C-suite framework. The leaders who got there in 2022 already have the advantage. The rest are catching up to something they're still calling soft.

charts.desc: McKinsey chart quantifying trauma impact on workforce performance. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: AI = Railway Boom. The Infrastructure Is Real. Most Railway Stocks Went to Zero.
items.idea: 'Big Tech spending has become the market's proof that the future is already under construction. The data centre, the power grid, the chip complex, the cooling systems, the fibre networks and the industrial supply chain have become the modern equivalent of the railway boom.' Real infrastructure is being built, real capital deployed, real bottlenecks emerging. But: 'every capex supercycle eventually encounters the same question: who pays, when do they earn it back, and what happens when the supply chain catches up?'
items.angle: The railway analogy is historically precise: the railroads transformed civilization AND most railway stocks went to zero. The returns went to the broader economy, not the equity holders. The core question for AI is who in the value chain actually captures returns: compute (NVDA), hyperscalers (MSFT/GOOG/AMZN), or the application layer.
items.tweetA: 'The AI data centre build is the modern equivalent of the railway boom.' Real infrastructure, real capital, real bottlenecks. The railways were also real — and most railway stocks went to zero. Infrastructure being built doesn't guarantee equities capture the returns.
items.tweetB: The capex supercycle question nobody wants to ask: who pays, when do they earn it back, and what happens when supply catches up? Right now the market just sees the cranes and backlogs. That's how every great supercycle looks at the beginning. And at the end.
